answer:
High. Nearly every factor in your description points to "employee," not contractor:

- **Company equipment** (your laptop) — cuts against independence under IRS control test and DOL's economic-realities "investment" factor.
- **Daily standups / set integration** — behavioral control over how and when she works.
- **Core product work** — the DOL 2024 rule and IRS both weigh "integral to the business" heavily against contractor status.
- **Hourly pay** — looks like wages, not project-based entrepreneurial risk/profit.
- **~30 hrs/week for a year** — permanence and likely economic dependence (probably little capacity for other clients).

Ohio adds its own exposure: the state uses a right-to-control test for unemployment insurance and a 20-factor test for workers' comp, and the Ohio BWC and ODJFS can audit independently of the IRS.

**What you're exposed to:**
- Back employer payroll taxes (FICA, FUTA) plus her share if she didn't pay SE tax, with penalties and interest; up to 3 years, more if willful
- Unpaid overtime under FLSA if she ever exceeded 40 hrs (2–3 year lookback, liquidated/double damages)
- Ohio unemployment contributions and workers' comp premiums, retroactive
- Benefits claims (retirement plan, health coverage eligibility) if she'd have qualified as a W-2

**What to do:** Reclassify her as a part-time W-2 employee going forward — the fact pattern is not realistically defensible. To clean up the past, look at the IRS Voluntary Classification Settlement Program (VCSP), which caps federal liability at roughly 1% of her past-year wages with no penalties, provided you've filed 1099s and aren't already under audit. Get employment counsel involved before an agency or the worker forces the issue (an SS-8 filing or unemployment claim by her would trigger review).
